enrichment.short_description: Open-source and cloud authorization platform for fine-grained RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC access control across apps, APIs, and AI agents.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cerbos: open-source authorization platform for fine-grained RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC access control across applications, APIs, and AI agents.

features.title: Multiple authorization models
features.description: Define policies using RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC, or PBAC models in a YAML-based policy language.

features.title: AI agent guardrails
features.description: Govern and revoke AI agent access to tools and actions in seconds, with support for the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

features.title: Cerbos Hub
features.description: Centralized policy administration point for managing, testing, and deploying policies across environments.

features.title: Cerbos Synapse
features.description: Enriches authorization decisions with context pulled from existing systems and data sources.

features.title: Sub-millisecond decision latency
features.description: Policy Decision Points (PDPs) are built for high-volume authorization checks with very low latency.

features.title: Audit-ready logging
features.description: Captures every authorization decision along with policy lineage for compliance and traceability.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Runs in Cerbos Hub (cloud), self-hosted, on-premise, or in air-gapped environments.

features.title: Broad SDK support
features.description: Client SDKs available for JavaScript, Python, Go, Rust, Java, .NET, PHP, and Ruby.

pros: Free, open-source core with unlimited monthly active principals (MAPs)
Supports RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC, and PBAC in a single policy engine
Can be deployed on-premise or air-gapped for regulated, high-security environments
Broad SDK/language coverage across major backend stacks
Purpose-built AI agent authorization features, including MCP integration
cons: Production tier starts at $933/month, a steep jump from the $25/month Development tier
Policy build counts and audit log retention are capped on lower-tier paid plans
SSO and self-hosted Cerbos Hub require moving to custom Enterprise pricing

faqs.question: Is Cerbos free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cerbos has a free, open-source version with unlimited monthly active principals, plus a free $0/month Proof of Concept tier for evaluating Cerbos Hub.

faqs.question: What authorization models does Cerbos support?
faqs.answer: Cerbos supports RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC, and PBAC policy models, defined in a YAML-based policy language.

faqs.question: Can Cerbos be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes. In addition to its managed Cerbos Hub cloud offering, Cerbos can run self-hosted, on-premise, or in air-gapped environments.

faqs.question: Does Cerbos support AI agent authorization?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cerbos provides AI agent guardrails for governing and revoking agent tool access, including integration with the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

faqs.question: How much does the Production plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Production plan starts from $933/month and includes the first 5,000 monthly active principals plus one year of unified audit logs.

use_cases.title: AI agent and MCP server authorization
use_cases.description: Govern which tools and actions AI agents can access, and revoke access quickly when behavior changes.
use_cases.audience: AI/ML engineering teams building agentic systems

use_cases.title: Multi-tenant SaaS access control
use_cases.description: Enforce per-tenant permission boundaries with centralized policy management via Cerbos Hub.

use_cases.title: Regulated industry compliance
use_cases.description: Deploy self-hosted or air-gapped authorization to meet SOC 2, HIPAA, or FedRAMP requirements.
use_cases.audience: Security and compliance teams in finance, healthcare, and government

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Cerbos?
paragraphs: Cerbos is an authorization platform that lets teams define and enforce fine-grained, contextual access control across applications, APIs, AI agents, and workloads. It supports RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC, and PBAC policy models written in a YAML-based policy language, and evaluates decisions through Policy Decision Points (PDPs) built for sub-millisecond latency.

The platform has an open-source core with unlimited monthly active principals, alongside a managed offering (Cerbos Hub) for centralized policy administration, plus paid tiers for teams that need larger scale, audit log retention, or enterprise features like SSO and self-hosted Hub deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Cerbos is for
paragraphs: Cerbos is aimed at engineering and security teams that need to move authorization logic out of application code, including teams building multi-tenant SaaS products, teams governing what AI agents and MCP tool calls are permitted to do, and organizations in regulated industries that require self-hosted or air-gapped deployment for compliance.

enrichment.short_description: Permit.io is a real-time authorization platform for RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC access control across apps and AI agents, built on OPA and OPAL.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Permit.io: real-time authorization and access control platform for apps, APIs, and AI agents, built on open-source OPA and OPAL.

features.title: MCP Gateway
features.description: Authenticates AI agents, gates tokens, collects consent, and governs tool access in real time.

features.title: Multi-model policy engine
features.description: Supports RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC with policy-as-code workflows.

features.title: Distributed Policy Decision Point (PDP)
features.description: Delivers sub-millisecond authorization decisions, deployable in-VPC or at the edge.

features.title: Elements
features.description: Embeddable UI components for building approval and access-request workflows into applications.

features.title: Audit logs and decision traces
features.description: Provides visibility across human-to-agent-to-tool-to-data authorization chains.

features.title: Guardian Agents
features.description: AI monitoring that watches for anomalous access behavior and recommends security policy changes.

features.title: Built on OPA and OPAL
features.description: Uses the open-source Open Policy Agent and OPAL projects as its policy engine foundation, available managed or self-hosted.

pros: Free Community plan with up to 1,000 monthly active users and unlimited authorization microservices
Supports RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC, and PBAC in one policy engine
Built on established open-source projects (OPA/OPAL) rather than a proprietary policy language
Offers both managed cloud and self-hosted/on-premises deployment
SOC 2 Type II certified, with HIPAA/GDPR/CCPA compliance options at the Enterprise tier
cons: SSO support requires an additional cost even on the paid Pro plan
Full compliance suite (HIPAA BAA, complete SOC 2 documentation) is limited to the custom Enterprise tier
Tenant and MAU limits on Community/Startup plans may require upgrading as usage grows

faqs.question: Is Permit.io free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Community plan is free forever for up to 1,000 monthly active users, 20 tenants, and 3 environments.

faqs.question: What authorization models does Permit.io support?
faqs.answer: RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC, and PBAC, managed through a UI and API alongside infrastructure-as-code workflows.

faqs.question: Can Permit.io be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes. Because it's built on the open-source OPA and OPAL projects, Permit.io supports both managed and self-hosted deployment, including on-premises for Enterprise customers.

faqs.question: Does Permit.io have AI-specific features?
faqs.answer: Yes. It offers an MCP Gateway for authenticating and governing AI agent tool access, plus Guardian Agents that monitor for anomalous behavior and recommend policy changes.

faqs.question: How is Permit.io priced?
faqs.answer: Plans scale by monthly active users and tenant count: Community is free, Startup starts from $5/month, Pro starts from $25/month, and Enterprise uses custom pricing.

use_cases.title: AI agent tool-access governance
use_cases.description: Use the MCP Gateway to authenticate agents and control which tools and actions they can invoke in real time.
use_cases.audience: Teams building AI agents and LLM tool integrations

use_cases.title: Multi-tenant SaaS permission management
use_cases.description: Manage per-tenant RBAC/ABAC/ReBAC permissions as products scale from B2C to B2B.
use_cases.audience: Product engineering teams at growing SaaS companies

use_cases.title: Regulated enterprise access control
use_cases.description: Deploy on-premises or multi-cloud with a full compliance suite for HIPAA, GDPR, CCPA, and SOC 2 requirements.
use_cases.audience: Enterprises in fintech, healthcare, and other regulated industries

features: UI and API access for RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC, PBAC, and IaC
Full GitOps CI/CD pipeline for automated deployments
Full compliance suite (HIPAA BAA, GDPR, CCPA, SOC 2)

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Permit.io?
paragraphs: Permit.io is an authorization and permissions management platform built on the open-source Open Policy Agent (OPA) and OPAL projects. It provides real-time, fine-grained access control using RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC, and PBAC models, with a distributed Policy Decision Point architecture designed for sub-millisecond decisions in-VPC or at the edge.

Beyond traditional application authorization, Permit.io markets itself around AI agent security: an MCP Gateway authenticates agents and governs their tool access in real time, while 'Guardian Agents' monitor behavior for anomalies and recommend policy adjustments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Permit.io is for
paragraphs: Permit.io targets engineering teams building B2C or B2B SaaS products that need scalable, policy-as-code authorization, as well as teams deploying AI agents that require real-time, action-level access governance. Its Enterprise tier, with a full HIPAA/GDPR/CCPA/SOC 2 compliance suite and on-premises deployment, is aimed at regulated organizations such as those in fintech and healthcare.

name: Aserto
skip_reason: Aserto is winding down as a commercial entity. Its own blog post 'The final chapter for Aserto' (aserto.com/blog/the-final-chapter-for-aserto) announces that the SaaS control plane is being sunset (shutdown by May 31, 2025) because the hosted business model could not be made profitable, and the company is discontinuing Aserto as a commercial product in favor of the open-source Topaz project ('Goodbye Aserto, long live Topaz'). No current pricing, plans, or company facts can be verified as still active under the Aserto name.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, CNCF-incubating engine for fine-grained, relationship-based authorization inspired by Google Zanzibar.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenFGA is a free, open-source, CNCF project for fine-grained, Zanzibar-style relationship-based access control (ReBAC).

features.title: Zanzibar-inspired ReBAC modeling
features.description: Uses an authorization modeling language, based on concepts from Google's Zanzibar paper, to define relationship-based access control between users and resources.

features.title: Millisecond authorization checks
features.description: Designed to answer authorization check calls in milliseconds, suitable for use in the request path of applications and APIs.

features.title: Multi-model support (RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC)
features.description: Supports role-based, attribute-based, and relationship-based access control patterns within the same system.

features.title: Multi-language SDKs
features.description: Official SDKs are available for Node.js, Go, .NET, Python, and Java, alongside a CLI and direct HTTP API access.

features.title: Self-hosted server via Docker
features.description: Runs as a standalone OpenFGA server that teams can deploy themselves, for example by pulling the official Docker image.

features.title: Relationship query API
features.description: Provides Check, Read, Expand, and ListObjects operations for querying and evaluating relationship tuples.

features.title: Community governance
features.description: Developed with a transparent RFC process and public roadmap, and maintained by contributors including engineers from Okta, Grafana Labs, and Gitpod.

pros: Free and fully open source, with no licensing cost
CNCF incubating project, providing vendor-neutral governance
Adopted in production by organizations such as Auth0, Okta, Docker, and Grafana
SDKs for several popular languages reduce integration effort
Flexible enough to model RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC in one system
cons: Requires self-hosting and operating your own OpenFGA server; openfga.dev does not document a managed hosted offering
Zanzibar-style relationship modeling has a learning curve for teams unfamiliar with the approach
As a community open-source project, formal commercial support is not part of the core openfga.dev offering

faqs.question: What is OpenFGA?
faqs.answer: OpenFGA is an open-source authorization system that lets developers implement fine-grained, relationship-based access control using an authorization modeling language and API.

faqs.question: Is OpenFGA free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. OpenFGA is free and open source, and can be self-hosted (for example via Docker) with no licensing fees.

faqs.question: Who maintains OpenFGA?
faqs.answer: OpenFGA is a CNCF incubating project. It was originally created by Auth0/Okta and open sourced in 2022, and is now maintained by a broader community including engineers from Grafana Labs and Gitpod.

faqs.question: What authorization models does OpenFGA support?
faqs.answer: OpenFGA supports relationship-based access control (ReBAC) inspired by Google's Zanzibar paper, as well as role-based (RBAC) and attribute-based (ABAC) access control patterns.

faqs.question: What SDKs does OpenFGA provide?
faqs.answer: OpenFGA offers SDKs for Node.js, Go, .NET, Python, and Java, in addition to a CLI and a direct HTTP API.

use_cases.title: Multi-tenant SaaS access control
use_cases.description: Model per-tenant roles and resource-level permissions so users only see and act on data they're authorized for.

use_cases.title: B2B application permissions
use_cases.description: Implement fine-grained permission checks for complex organizational structures, teams, and shared resources in business applications.
use_cases.audience: Platform and backend engineers building B2B software

use_cases.title: Replacing hardcoded authorization logic
use_cases.description: Move authorization decisions out of application code and into a centralized, queryable relationship-based model.
use_cases.audience: Development teams migrating from ad hoc or hardcoded RBAC checks

features: Full relationship-query API access (Check, Read, Expand, ListObjects)
SDKs for Node.js, Go, .NET, Python, and Java

long_form_blocks.heading: What is OpenFGA?
paragraphs: OpenFGA is an open-source authorization system for implementing fine-grained access control, using a modeling language and API inspired by Google's Zanzibar paper on relationship-based access control (ReBAC).

Originally created by Auth0/Okta and open sourced in 2022, OpenFGA is now a CNCF incubating project maintained by a community that includes engineers from Okta, Grafana Labs, and Gitpod, with adopters such as Auth0, Docker, and Grafana.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who OpenFGA is for
paragraphs: OpenFGA is aimed at developers and platform teams who need to move authorization logic out of application code, particularly for multi-tenant SaaS products and B2B applications with complex, relationship-driven permission requirements.

enrichment.short_description: SpiceDB is an open-source, Google Zanzibar-inspired database for building fine-grained, relationship-based application permissions.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SpiceDB is an open-source, Zanzibar-inspired permissions database from AuthZed for fine-grained, relationship-based authorization.

features.title: Expressive APIs
features.description: Exposes gRPC and HTTP/JSON APIs for defining and checking authorization logic from any application stack.

features.title: Distributed Graph Engine
features.description: A parallel, distributed architecture based on Google's Zanzibar paper for evaluating permission checks at scale.

features.title: Configurable Consistency
features.description: Per-request consistency settings designed to resist the 'New Enemy Problem' common in permission systems.

features.title: SpiceDB Schema Language
features.description: A dedicated schema language for modeling permissions, with support for CI/CD integration and validation.

features.title: Pluggable Storage
features.description: Supports in-memory storage plus production datastores including PostgreSQL, MySQL, CockroachDB, and Google Cloud Spanner.

features.title: Deep Observability
features.description: Built-in Prometheus metrics, pprof profiles, structured logging, and OpenTelemetry tracing.

features.title: zed CLI and SpiceDB Operator
features.description: A command-line tool for managing SpiceDB deployments plus a Kubernetes operator for cluster management.

pros: Open source under the Apache-2.0 license with no vendor lock-in
Implements Google's proven Zanzibar model for large-scale, fine-grained authorization
Supports multiple production-grade storage backends (Postgres, CockroachDB, MySQL, Spanner)
Used in production by companies including Reddit and Netflix
Includes built-in observability tooling (Prometheus, OpenTelemetry, pprof)
cons: Zanzibar-style relationship-based access control has a learning curve for teams new to the model
Self-hosting requires operating a distributed system and managing a separate storage backend
Self-hosted enterprise licensing and Dedicated Cloud pricing require a custom quote rather than public pricing

faqs.question: Is SpiceDB open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, SpiceDB is licensed under Apache 2.0 and can be self-hosted for free.

faqs.question: What storage backends does SpiceDB support?
faqs.answer: SpiceDB supports in-memory storage as well as PostgreSQL, MySQL, CockroachDB, and Google Cloud Spanner as pluggable datastores.

faqs.question: Does AuthZed offer a managed version of SpiceDB?
faqs.answer: Yes, AuthZed Cloud is a self-service managed hosting option billed on a pay-as-you-go basis starting around $2/hr, alongside AuthZed Dedicated and Self-Hosted enterprise licensing.

faqs.question: What companies use SpiceDB in production?
faqs.answer: According to AuthZed, SpiceDB is used in production by companies including Reddit and Netflix.

faqs.question: What APIs does SpiceDB expose?
faqs.answer: SpiceDB exposes both gRPC and HTTP/JSON APIs for defining and evaluating authorization logic.

use_cases.title: Fine-grained application permissions
use_cases.description: Teams building SaaS applications that need relationship-based access control (e.g., who can view or edit a document) can model permission hierarchies using SpiceDB's schema language.

use_cases.title: Zanzibar-style authorization at scale
use_cases.description: Organizations needing low-latency, consistent authorization checks across a distributed graph can deploy SpiceDB's distributed graph engine.

use_cases.title: Kubernetes-native permission deployments
use_cases.description: Teams running Kubernetes can use the SpiceDB Operator to manage SpiceDB clusters alongside their existing infrastructure.

features: Community support via Discord and GitHub

long_form_blocks.heading: What is SpiceDB?
paragraphs: SpiceDB is an open-source permissions database inspired by Google's Zanzibar paper, built to help engineering teams implement fine-grained, relationship-based access control (ReBAC) instead of hand-rolling custom authorization logic.

It exposes gRPC and HTTP/JSON APIs backed by a distributed graph engine, and supports pluggable storage backends including PostgreSQL, MySQL, CockroachDB, and Google Cloud Spanner. AuthZed, the company behind SpiceDB, also offers managed hosting through AuthZed Cloud and AuthZed Dedicated, plus a licensed self-hosted enterprise edition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who SpiceDB is for
paragraphs: SpiceDB is aimed at backend and platform engineering teams that need to model complex, evolving permission structures across an application — from simple role checks to nested organizational hierarchies — with a system proven in production at companies like Reddit and Netflix.

name: BoxyHQ
skip_reason: boxyhq.com now redirects (301) to ory.com. BoxyHQ was acquired by Ory in May 2025 and its flagship SSO/SCIM product (formerly BoxyHQ Jackson) has been rebranded as 'Ory Polis'. The independent BoxyHQ product/brand no longer exists at its official domain, so no current facts can be verified under the BoxyHQ name.

enrichment.short_description: WorkOS is an API platform providing enterprise SSO, directory sync, RBAC, and user management for B2B SaaS applications.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: WorkOS provides enterprise SSO, directory sync, and user management APIs so B2B SaaS companies can sell to enterprise customers.

features.title: Enterprise SSO
features.description: Unified single sign-on integration supporting 20+ identity providers, including Okta, Azure AD, Google, OneLogin, PingIdentity, ADFS, and JumpCloud, via SAML and OIDC.

features.title: Directory Sync
features.description: Automated user lifecycle management by syncing users and groups from corporate directories and HR systems such as BambooHR.

features.title: AuthKit
features.description: A customizable, prebuilt authentication UI covering email/password login, social login, passkeys, magic auth, and MFA.

features.title: Admin Portal
features.description: A self-serve portal that lets corporate IT admins configure their own SSO and directory connections without vendor involvement.

features.title: RBAC
features.description: Role-based access control for defining and managing flexible permissions across users and organizations.

features.title: Audit Logs
features.description: Event logging with export and streaming support, including SIEM connections, for compliance and security monitoring.

features.title: Radar
features.description: Real-time protection against bots, fraud, and abuse, with the first 1,000 checks included free each month.

features.title: Multi-language SDKs
features.description: Official SDKs for Node.js, Ruby, Python, Go, PHP, Java, and .NET, plus a REST API with cURL examples.

pros: AuthKit is free for the first 1 million monthly active users in production
Broad enterprise identity provider coverage with 20+ supported providers
Tiered connection pricing includes automatic volume discounts as usage scales
Staging environments are always free regardless of usage
Used in production by companies including Vercel, OpenAI, Cursor, and Perplexity
cons: SSO and Directory Sync are billed per connection starting at $125 each, which can add up for B2B apps serving many enterprise customers
Pricing above 200 connections requires contacting sales, so costs at scale aren't fully public
Guaranteed support SLAs and video call support require the $1,000/month Scale plan or a custom Enterprise plan
Audit Logs and Custom Domain are separately priced add-ons rather than included by default

faqs.question: Does WorkOS have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. AuthKit is free for the first 1 million monthly active users in production, and staging environments are always free.

faqs.question: How is Single Sign-On priced on WorkOS?
faqs.answer: SSO and Directory Sync are billed per connection on a tiered scale, from $125/connection for 1-15 connections down to $50/connection for 101-200 connections, with custom pricing above 200 connections.

faqs.question: What identity providers does WorkOS support?
faqs.answer: WorkOS integrates with 20+ identity providers, including Okta, Azure AD, Google, OneLogin, PingIdentity, ADFS, and JumpCloud.

faqs.question: Does WorkOS provide fraud or bot protection?
faqs.answer: Yes, through a feature called Radar, which offers real-time protection against bots, fraud, and abuse, with the first 1,000 checks free each month.

faqs.question: What SDKs and languages does WorkOS support?
faqs.answer: WorkOS provides SDKs for Node.js, Ruby, Python, Go, PHP, Java, and .NET, along with a REST API.

use_cases.title: Becoming enterprise-ready
use_cases.description: Adding SSO, SCIM directory sync, RBAC, and an admin portal so a SaaS product meets the security and provisioning requirements of enterprise procurement.
use_cases.audience: B2B SaaS engineering teams selling to enterprise customers

use_cases.title: Fraud and bot mitigation
use_cases.description: Using Radar to detect and block bots, fraud, and abusive traffic in real time.
use_cases.audience: Security and platform teams protecting sign-up and login flows

use_cases.title: Prebuilt authentication UI
use_cases.description: Using AuthKit to launch passwordless, social, and MFA-enabled login flows without building auth screens from scratch.
use_cases.audience: Early-stage startups and developer teams that want to ship auth quickly

pricing_plans.price: Free up to 1M MAU, then $2,500/month per additional 1M MAU
$125 per connection (1-15), decreasing to $50 per connection (101-200); custom above 200

long_form_blocks.heading: What is WorkOS?
paragraphs: WorkOS is an API platform that helps developers add enterprise-grade authentication and identity features to their applications. Its core products include Enterprise SSO, Directory Sync, an Admin Portal, RBAC, Audit Logs, and AuthKit, a prebuilt authentication UI covering passwords, social login, passkeys, magic auth, and MFA.

The platform connects to more than 20 identity providers, including Okta, Azure AD, Google, OneLogin, PingIdentity, ADFS, and JumpCloud, and offers SDKs for Node.js, Ruby, Python, Go, PHP, Java, and .NET alongside a REST API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who WorkOS is for
paragraphs: WorkOS is built for B2B SaaS engineering teams that need to satisfy enterprise procurement and IT requirements, such as SSO, SCIM directory sync, and audit logging, without building that infrastructure themselves. Companies referenced as customers on the WorkOS site include Vercel, OpenAI, Cursor, Perplexity, and Netlify.

enrichment.short_description: Stytch is an identity platform providing authentication, authorization, and fraud prevention APIs for humans and AI agents.
enrichment.starting_price: $0/month (Pay As You Go, free up to 10,000 MAUs)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Stytch offers authentication, SSO, and fraud prevention APIs for human users and AI agents, with a free tier up to 10,000 MAUs.

features.title: Enterprise SSO & SCIM
features.description: SAML and OIDC single sign-on plus SCIM provisioning, with the first 5 SSO/SCIM connections included free.

features.title: Passwordless authentication
features.description: Passkeys, magic links, and one-time passcodes delivered via SMS and email, with automatic provider failover for delivery reliability.

features.title: Machine-to-machine (M2M) authentication
features.description: Authentication for service-to-service calls and AI agents, with 1,000 M2M tokens included in the free tier.

features.title: Fraud and bot protection
features.description: Device fingerprinting and intelligent rate limiting, described as achieving 99.99% bot detection accuracy, with 10,000 free fingerprints per month.

features.title: Turnkey multi-tenancy
features.description: Organization-level policies, RBAC, and SCIM support for building B2B applications with unlimited organizations, even on the free tier.

features.title: Embeddable Admin Portal
features.description: A self-service portal that lets enterprise customers manage their own SSO and SCIM connections.

features.title: Connected Apps
features.description: Support for cross-application integrations between a company's own products and third-party apps.

features.title: Web & mobile UI components
features.description: Prebuilt, customizable login UI components alongside headless SDKs and backend APIs for full flexibility.

pros: Free tier includes 10,000 monthly active users/AI agents, unlimited organizations, 5 SSO/SCIM connections, and 1,000 M2M tokens
Pricing has no hard caps or pricing cliffs, so overages are billed for actual usage only
Explicit support for machine-to-machine and AI agent authentication, not just human end users
Broad auth method coverage including passkeys, OAuth, SSO, sessions, and RBAC in the base tier
Backed by Twilio after Stytch joined the company, adding messaging infrastructure depth
cons: SSO/SCIM connections beyond the free 5 cost $125 per connection, similar to competitors
Enterprise pricing, including HIPAA/BAA compliance and a 99.99% uptime SLA, is custom and not publicly listed
Brand customization requires a separate $99 one-time fee
Fraud prevention fingerprinting beyond the free 10,000 is billed per use at $0.005 each

faqs.question: Is Stytch free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Pay As You Go plan is free up to 10,000 monthly active users/AI agents, 5 SSO or SCIM connections, and 1,000 M2M tokens.

faqs.question: Does Stytch support authentication for AI agents?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stytch positions itself as an identity platform for both humans and AI agents, and its free tier explicitly covers AI agents alongside human monthly active users.

faqs.question: What happens if usage exceeds the free tier?
faqs.answer: Stytch charges usage-based overages with no hard caps or pricing cliffs, plus $125 per additional SSO/SCIM connection beyond the 5 included free.

faqs.question: Is Stytch part of Twilio?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stytch has joined Twilio to build what the company describes as the intelligent identity layer for the internet.

faqs.question: Does Stytch offer fraud protection?
faqs.answer: Yes, via device fingerprinting (10,000 free per month, then $0.005 each) and intelligent rate limiting for bot detection.

use_cases.title: B2B multi-tenant applications
use_cases.description: Using turnkey multi-tenancy, organization policies, and SSO/SCIM to serve enterprise customers with their own auth requirements.

use_cases.title: AI agent authentication
use_cases.description: Issuing M2M tokens to authenticate AI agents and backend services rather than only human users.
use_cases.audience: Teams building AI-agent products that need machine identity

use_cases.title: Consumer passwordless login
use_cases.description: Implementing passkeys, magic links, and OTP delivery to reduce password-related friction in consumer sign-up and login flows.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Stytch?
paragraphs: Stytch is an identity platform offering authentication, authorization, and security APIs, including SSO, SCIM, passkeys, OTP, and machine-to-machine authentication. The company describes its product as built for both human users and AI agents.

Core features include an embeddable admin portal for enterprise self-service, device fingerprinting and rate limiting for fraud prevention, turnkey multi-tenancy with organization policies, and prebuilt web and mobile UI components alongside headless SDKs and backend APIs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Stytch is for
paragraphs: Stytch is aimed at development teams building B2B or consumer applications that need enterprise-grade authentication, including SSO/SCIM for enterprise customers and passwordless login for end users. Its free tier and explicit support for authenticating AI agents also make it relevant to teams building AI-agent products that need machine-to-machine identity.

enrichment.short_description: Descope is a customer identity platform offering no-code auth flows, fine-grained authorization, and identity security for apps and AI agents.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Descope offers passwordless login, MFA, RBAC/ReBAC/ABAC, multi-tenancy, and AI agent/MCP authorization with a no-code Flows builder.

features.title: Descope Flows
features.description: A visual, drag-and-drop workflow builder for designing authentication and identity journeys without hand-coding custom auth logic.

features.title: Multiple authentication methods
features.description: Supports passwordless login, OTP, magic links, social login, SSO, and adaptive MFA.

features.title: Fine-grained authorization
features.description: Role-based (RBAC), relationship-based (ReBAC), and attribute-based (ABAC) access control models.

features.title: Multi-tenancy
features.description: Organization and tenant management with delegated admin for B2B customers.

features.title: AI agent and MCP server security
features.description: Authorization, scope-based access control, consent, token management, and DCR security purpose-built for AI agents and MCP servers.

features.title: Broad SDK and framework support
features.description: SDKs for React, Next.js, Vue.js, Angular, Python, Node.js, Java, .NET, Go, PHP, Ruby, Kotlin, Swift, React Native, and Flutter.

features.title: Adaptive, risk-based security controls
features.description: Risk signals and adaptive MFA that adjust authentication requirements based on user behavior.

features.title: Third-party integrations
features.description: Connects with 50+ third-party tools as part of its identity orchestration.

pros: Free Forever tier includes 7,500 MAUs, 10 tenants, and 3 SSO connections with no time limit
No-code visual Flows builder reduces the need for custom authentication code
Supports advanced authorization models (RBAC/ReBAC/ABAC) beyond basic login
Wide SDK/language coverage across web, backend, and mobile frameworks
Purpose-built support for securing AI agents and MCP servers
cons: Paid plans include usage-based MAU overages that can add cost as user base grows
SCIM provisioning and bot protection require the Growth tier or higher
Enterprise pricing is quote-only and not publicly listed
Free tier is capped at 10 tenants and 3 SSO connections

faqs.question: Does Descope offer a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, its Free Forever plan includes 7,500 MAUs, 10 tenants, and 3 SSO connections at no cost.

faqs.question: What is Descope Flows?
faqs.answer: A visual, drag-and-drop workflow builder for designing authentication and identity journeys without writing custom auth code.

faqs.question: Does Descope support AI agent authentication?
faqs.answer: Yes, Descope provides authorization, scope-based access control, and consent and token management for AI agents and MCP servers.

faqs.question: What authorization models does Descope support?
faqs.answer: Descope supports role-based (RBAC), relationship-based (ReBAC), and attribute-based (ABAC) access control.

faqs.question: How much does Descope's Pro plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Pro plan starts at $249/month (billed annually) and includes 10,000 MAUs and 5 SSO connections.

use_cases.title: Customer identity and access management for SaaS apps
use_cases.description: Add login, MFA, and identity flows to customer-facing applications using prebuilt auth methods.

use_cases.title: B2B multi-tenant authentication
use_cases.description: Manage delegated admin, SSO, and per-tenant configuration for business customers.

use_cases.title: Securing AI agents and MCP servers
use_cases.description: Apply authorization, consent, and token management to AI agents accessing internal systems.

use_cases.title: No-code auth journey design
use_cases.description: Build and iterate on login flows visually without hand-coding integrations.

features: Unlimited test users and anonymous users

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Descope?
paragraphs: Descope is a customer identity and access management (CIAM) platform for building authentication, authorization, and identity workflows.

Its Descope Flows builder lets teams design login journeys visually, while its authorization layer supports RBAC, ReBAC, and ABAC models. Descope also markets specific support for securing AI agents and MCP servers, including scope-based access control, consent management, and token management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Descope is for
paragraphs: Descope suits engineering teams building customer-facing or B2B applications that need flexible authentication and multi-tenant access control, from solo developers on its free tier to enterprises needing SCIM provisioning and dedicated support.

enrichment.short_description: Kinde is a developer platform that combines authentication, user management, feature flags, and billing to help teams build and monetize SaaS products.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kinde combines auth, B2B org management, feature flags, and built-in subscription billing on one platform, with a free plan up to 10,500 MAU.

features.title: Authentication and MFA
features.description: Login, multi-factor authentication, and custom domain support for user-facing auth flows.

features.title: User management and organizations
features.description: Multi-tenant organization support for B2B products, including roles and permissions.

features.title: Feature flags
features.description: Control feature rollout without redeploying code.

features.title: Built-in billing and monetization
features.description: Subscription billing tied directly to user authentication and access.

features.title: Access management
features.description: Roles, permissions, and API scopes to control what authenticated users can do.

features.title: Extensive SDK support
features.description: SDKs for .NET, Java, Node.js, Python, Ruby, PHP, Elixir, React, JavaScript, iOS, Android, Flutter, and React Native.

features.title: Enterprise SSO and SCIM
features.description: Unlimited enterprise SSO from the Plus plan, with SCIM provisioning on the Scale plan.

features.title: Custom branding and domains
features.description: Customize the look of authentication pages and use custom domains.

pros: Free plan supports up to 10,500 MAU with no credit card required
Built-in billing/monetization reduces need for a separate billing integration
Transparent, publicly listed pricing across all four tiers
Free MAU for users on paid billing subscriptions
Broad SDK coverage across backend, frontend, and mobile frameworks
cons: Transaction fees (0.5%-0.7%) apply on top of subscription cost when using billing features
Enterprise SSO only available starting on the $75/month Plus plan
SCIM provisioning requires the top-tier $250/month Scale plan
Environment counts are capped even on paid plans (e.g., 6 on Plus, 11 on Scale)

faqs.question: Does Kinde have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Free plan supports up to 10,500 MAU with no credit card required.

faqs.question: How much does Kinde cost?
faqs.answer: Plans range from Free ($0) to Pro ($25/month), Plus ($75/month), and Scale ($250/month), all billed monthly.

faqs.question: Does Kinde charge transaction fees?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kinde charges a transaction fee on billing plans that decreases from 0.7% on Free/Pro to 0.5% on Scale.

faqs.question: Does Kinde support enterprise SSO?
faqs.answer: Yes, unlimited enterprise SSO is included starting on the Plus plan ($75/month).

faqs.question: What SDKs does Kinde support?
faqs.answer: Kinde provides SDKs for .NET, Java, Node.js, Python, Ruby, PHP, Elixir, React, JavaScript, iOS, Android, Flutter, and React Native.

use_cases.title: Auth and billing for SaaS startups
use_cases.description: Combine login and subscription billing in a single platform instead of stitching together separate tools.

use_cases.title: B2B organization management
use_cases.description: Manage multi-tenant organizations with roles and permissions for business customers.

use_cases.title: Feature flag rollouts
use_cases.description: Control feature releases and gradual rollouts without redeploying code.

use_cases.title: Enterprise-ready authentication
use_cases.description: Add SSO, SCIM provisioning, and compliance reporting to sell to larger customers.

features: Custom domains and email senders per organization

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Kinde?
paragraphs: Kinde is a developer platform that bundles authentication, user management, feature flags, and billing into one product for building and monetizing SaaS applications.
It offers a free plan for up to 10,500 monthly active users, with paid tiers adding unlimited MAU, enterprise SSO, SCIM provisioning, and compliance reporting as needs grow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Kinde is for
paragraphs: Kinde suits SaaS teams that want authentication, B2B organization management, and subscription billing handled by a single platform rather than integrating separate auth and billing providers, scaling from free-tier startups to enterprise customers needing SSO and SCIM.
